content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 24, 1999

Burnham E. Matthews
Chief of Police
Alameda Police Department
1555 Oak Street
Alameda, California 94501

Re: Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-99-276

Dear Mr. Matthews:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice for you and Juelle-Ann Boyer regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

     You and Ms. Boyer are city employees who also serve as members of the board of directors of a local nonprofit corporation. Does a conflict arise if the nonprofit corporation negotiates a contract with a local school district?

CONCLUSION

     No. Neither you nor Ms. Boyer has a conflict of interest in the board of education's decision regarding the contract since you nor Ms. Boyer has an economic interest in the decision. But other laws outside of the Act, such as Government Code Section 1090, might apply.

FACTS

     You and Juelle-Ann Boyer are city officials. You are the city's chief of police and Ms. Boyer is a senior manager at Alameda Power & Telecom, a city-owned electric provider. Both you and Ms. Boyer sit on the board of directors of a nonprofit corporation called Xanthos,

_________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-99-276
Page 2

Inc. ("Xanthos"). Ms. Boyer is currently the president of the board. Neither you nor Ms. Boyer
receives monetary compensation from Xanthos.

Xanthos is a social services entity that was formed 28 years ago by a local citizen. It
receives most of its funding through federal grants. You are not aware of any laws that treat
Xanthos as a public entity. Among other things, Xanthos implements the federally funded Head
Start program. Head Start is a national program that provides services for America's low-income
preschool children, and social services for their families. The federal government created the
Head Start program in 1965. Through its regional offices, the Administration for Children and
Families awards grants for local Head Start programs. Head start grantees must be either public
or private nonprofit organizations or public school systems.

Recently, Xanthos negotiated a contract with the Alameda Unified School District
allowing Xanthos' Head Start program to be housed at an existing school site. The Alameda
Unified School District Board of Education is currently considering the contract. Neither you
nor Ms. Boyer is a member of the board of education.

ANALYSIS

A. Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition

A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his or
her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official knows, or has
reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) The Commission has
developed an eight-step approach for determining whether an individual has a disqualifying
financial interest in a decision. (See Regulation 18700(b).) The steps relevant to your request
are discussed below.

1. Public Official

The conflict-of-interest prohibition only applies to public officials. (Section 87100.) The
term "public official" means "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
governmental agency." (Section 82048.) As a police chief, you are a public official subject to
the prohibition. As a senior manager with a city-owned utility, Ms. Boyer is also a public
official subject to the prohibition.

An individual can also be a public official by sitting on the board of directors of a
nonprofit corporation if the entity is a "local governmental agency" under the Act. (Section
82041.) To determine whether a nonprofit corporation is a public agency, the Commission has
devised four factors:

File No. A-99-276
Page 3

1. Whether the impetus for formation of the entity originated with a government agency;
2. Whether the entity is substantially funded by a governmental agency;
3. Whether the principal purpose of the entity is to provide services or undertake
obligations that public agencies traditionally perform; and
4. Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other statutory provisions.
(See In re Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62.)

Starting with the first factor, your facts indicate that Xanthos was formed by a private
citizen. Therefore, Xanthos does not meet the first factor. As to the second factor, your facts
mention that Xanthos receives most of its funding from the federal government. We have taken
the position in previous advice letters that if an entity receives most of its funding from federal
grant monies, it meets the second factor. (Schofield Advice Letter, No. A-89-540; Prestige
Advice Letter, No. A-95-323.) Thus, Xanthos meets the second factor.

With regard to the third factor, your facts indicate that from the very beginning both
public and private entities have implemented local Head Start programs. Since both public and
private entities have historically been involved in Head Start, the third factor is not conclusive.
Finally, you are not aware of any laws that treat Xanthos as a public entity.

After weighing these factors, we conclude that the first and fourth criteria weigh against a
finding that Xanthos is a local governmental agency, while the third factor is inconclusive. Since
Xanthos only meets one of the four criteria, it is not a local governmental agency. As such, you
and Ms. Boyer are not public officials by virtue of your positions with Xanthos.

2. Conduct Covered

The prohibition covers specific conduct: making, participating in making, or attempting
to use one's official position to influence a governmental decision. These terms are defined in
Regulations 18702-18702.4. To make or participate in making a governmental decision, an
official must be acting within the authority of his or her office or position. (Regulations 18702,
18702.2.) Since the contract decision is before the board of education, neither you nor
Ms. Boyer will be making or participating in making a governmental decision.

For decisions that are not before an official's agency, or an agency appointed by or
subject to the budgetary control of the official's agency, the official is attempting to use his or
her official position to influence the decision if the official acts or purports to act on behalf of, or
as the representative of, his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

Accordingly, you may not act or purport to act on behalf of or as a representative of the
police department in the contract decision before the board of education if you have a financial
interest in the decision. In addition, Ms. Boyer may not act or purport to act on behalf of or as a
representative of the utility company in the decision if she has a financial interest in the decision.

File No. A-99-276
Page 4

3. Economic Interest

An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official,² or on the following enumerated economic interests:

1. Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $1,000 or more.
2. Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or more.
3. Any source of income of $250 or more provided to the official within 12 months before the decision.
4. Any business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
5. Any donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the official within 12 months before the decision. (Section 87103(a)-(e).)

As voluntary members of Xanthos' board of directors, neither you nor Ms. Boyer has an economic interest in Xanthos. You have not identified any other economic interests that will be affected by the contract decision before the board of education. Therefore, neither you nor Ms. Boyer has a financial interest in this decision.

Although you do not have a conflict of interest under the Act, you may wish to consult the city attorney or private legal counsel regarding other conflict-of-interest laws outside the Act, such as Government Code Section 1090. If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By:    Julia Bilaver
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

LM:JB:tls

² A decision will have a financial effect "on the official" if the decision affects his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103.) This is known as the "personal financial effect" rule. (Regulation 18703.5.)

CONCLUSION: No. Neither you nor Ms. Boyer has a conflict of interest in the board of education’s
decision regarding the contract since you nor Ms. Boyer has an economic interest in the decision.
But other laws outside of the Act, such as Government Code Section 1090, might apply.
